projects.title: Function Declarations vs Function Expressions
projects.description: Compare hoisting behavior of function declarations and function expressions.
projects.exampleText:
console.log(declaration()); function declaration() { return 'Hoisted!'; }
const expression = function() { return 'Not hoisted!'; };
projects.exampleOutput: Hoisted!; TypeError: expression is not a function
projects.learningOutcome: Understanding hoisting and the difference between declaration and expression.
projects.logicExplanation: Function declarations are hoisted and can be called before definition, while function expressions are not hoisted and are assigned to variables. Declarations are useful for global functions, expressions are often used as callbacks.
projects.codeExplanation: The code shows a function declaration (hoisted) and a function expression (assigned to a variable). It demonstrates that the declaration can be called above its definition, while the expression cannot.

projects.title: Parameters, Arguments, and Default Parameters
projects.description: Demonstrate function parameters, passing arguments, and default parameter values.
projects.exampleText: function greet(name = 'Guest') { return `Hello, ${name}`; }
projects.logicExplanation: Parameters are placeholders in function definition; arguments are actual values passed. Default parameters allow setting fallback values if an argument is omitted or undefined.
projects.codeExplanation: The script defines a function with parameters, uses default values, and shows how arguments are matched to parameters.

projects.title: Return Values and Early Returns
projects.description: Show how return values work and how early returns can simplify logic.
projects.exampleText: function divide(a, b) { if (b === 0) return 'Error: division by zero'; return a / b; }
projects.learningOutcome: Returning values and using early returns.
projects.logicExplanation: Functions return values using `return`. If no `return` is present, the function returns `undefined`. Early returns can exit a function early based on conditions, avoiding nested logic.
projects.codeExplanation: The code has a function that uses early returns to handle invalid inputs and returns a computed result otherwise.

projects.title: Function Scope: Local vs Global Variables
projects.description: Illustrate the difference between global and local variables inside a function.
projects.exampleText:
let globalVar = 'global';
function testScope() { let localVar = 'local'; console.log(globalVar, localVar); }
projects.logicExplanation: Variables declared inside a function with `var`, `let`, or `const` are local to that function. Global variables are accessible everywhere, but local variables shadow globals within the function scope.
projects.codeExplanation: The script demonstrates a global variable and a local variable with the same name, showing how the local shadows the global.

projects.title: Arrow Functions and Concise Syntax
projects.description: Compare traditional functions with arrow functions, focusing on syntax and this binding.
projects.exampleText:
const add = (a, b) => a + b;
const obj = { name: 'Arrow', method: () => this.name };
projects.learningOutcome: Arrow function syntax and lexical this.
projects.logicExplanation: Arrow functions provide a shorter syntax, do not have their own `this`, and are not hoisted. They are ideal for short callbacks and when lexical `this` is needed.
projects.codeExplanation: The code compares a traditional function with an arrow function. It shows the syntax differences and how `this` behaves.

projects.title: Pure Functions vs Side Effects
projects.description: Define pure and impure functions, and understand the concept of side effects.
projects.exampleOutput: pure(1,2) always 3; impure modifies external state.
projects.learningOutcome: Identifying pure functions and side effects.
projects.logicExplanation: Pure functions always return the same output for the same input and cause no side effects (e.g., no mutation of external state). Impure functions may modify variables outside their scope or rely on external state.
projects.codeExplanation: The script defines a pure function that adds two numbers, and an impure function that modifies a global variable.

projects.title: Function Hoisting
projects.description: Explore how function declarations are hoisted, while expressions are not.
projects.exampleOutput: Works; Error if notHoisted called before assignment
projects.logicExplanation: Hoisting moves function declarations to the top of their scope. Function expressions are not hoisted; only the variable declaration is hoisted, but the assignment remains in place.
projects.codeExplanation: The code calls a function declared later (works) and attempts to call a function expression before its assignment (fails).

projects.title: Immediately Invoked Function Expressions (IIFE)
projects.description: Create and execute an IIFE to encapsulate code and avoid polluting global scope.
projects.exampleText: (function() { var private = 10; console.log(private); })();
projects.logicExplanation: IIFEs are functions that are defined and executed immediately. They create a new scope, avoiding polluting the global namespace, and are often used for module patterns.
projects.codeExplanation: The script creates an IIFE that executes instantly and returns a value, and shows how variables inside are not accessible globally.

projects.title: Callback Functions
projects.description: Pass a function as an argument to another function and execute it later.
projects.exampleText: function processUserInput(callback) { callback('John'); }
projects.logicExplanation: Callbacks are functions passed as arguments to other functions, to be executed later. They are fundamental to asynchronous programming and higher-order functions.
projects.codeExplanation: The code defines a function that accepts a callback and invokes it, demonstrating both named and anonymous callbacks.

projects.title: Higher-Order Functions
projects.description: Create a function that takes another function as an argument or returns a function.
projects.exampleText: function applyTwice(f, x) { return f(f(x)); }
projects.logicExplanation: Higher-order functions are functions that take other functions as arguments or return a function. Array methods like `map`, `filter`, and `reduce` are common examples.
projects.codeExplanation: The script creates a custom higher-order function that accepts a function and applies it to each element of an array.

projects.title: Function Rest Parameters
projects.description: Use rest parameters to handle an indefinite number of arguments as an array.
projects.exampleText: function sum(...numbers) { return numbers.reduce((a,b) => a+b, 0); }
projects.logicExplanation: Rest parameters allow a function to accept an indefinite number of arguments as an array. They are denoted by `...` before the parameter.
projects.codeExplanation: The code defines a function that uses rest parameters to sum any number of arguments, showing the array conversion.

projects.title: Function Spread Operator
projects.description: Spread array elements as arguments to a function.
projects.exampleText: const nums = [1,2,3]; Math.max(...nums);
projects.logicExplanation: The spread operator (`...`) expands an iterable (like an array) into individual arguments. It is useful for passing array elements to functions that expect separate parameters.
projects.codeExplanation: The script uses spread to pass array elements to a function that normally takes separate arguments.

projects.title: Closure Basics
projects.description: Create a closure where an inner function retains access to outer variables.
projects.exampleText: function outer(x) { return function inner(y) { return x + y; }; }
projects.logicExplanation: A closure is formed when a function retains access to variables from its outer scope even after the outer function has returned. It's used for data privacy and creating factories.
projects.codeExplanation: The code creates a function that returns an inner function, and the inner function remembers the outer variable, demonstrating closure.

projects.title: Function Factories (Returning Functions)
projects.description: Write a function that returns another function, often used to create customized functions.
projects.exampleText: function multiplier(factor) { return function(x) { return x * factor; }; }
projects.logicExplanation: Function factories are functions that return other functions, often used to create customized functions with preset parameters.
projects.codeExplanation: The script defines a multiplier factory that takes a factor and returns a function that multiplies a number by that factor.

projects.title: Recursion – Factorial
projects.description: Implement factorial using recursion.
projects.exampleText: function factorial(n) { return n <= 1 ? 1 : n * factorial(n-1); }
projects.logicExplanation: Recursion is when a function calls itself. The factorial function (n! = n * (n-1)!) is a classic recursive example.
projects.codeExplanation: The code implements factorial recursively, with a base case for 0 and 1, and shows the call stack.

projects.title: Recursion – Fibonacci
projects.description: Compute Fibonacci numbers recursively.
projects.exampleText: function fib(n) { return n <= 1 ? n : fib(n-1) + fib(n-2); }
projects.logicExplanation: The Fibonacci sequence is defined recursively: fib(n) = fib(n-1) + fib(n-2). Recursion can be expensive without memoization.
projects.codeExplanation: The script computes Fibonacci numbers recursively and notes performance issues with large n.

projects.title: Function Methods: call, apply, bind
projects.description: Use call, apply, and bind to control the value of this and arguments.
projects.exampleText:
function greet() { console.log(this.name); }
const obj = { name: 'Alice' };
projects.logicExplanation: `call` and `apply` invoke a function with a specified `this` and arguments. `bind` returns a new function with a bound `this`.
projects.codeExplanation: The code demonstrates using `call` and `apply` to invoke a function with different contexts, and uses `bind` to create a bound function.

projects.title: Function Arguments Object
projects.description: Access the arguments object inside a regular function (non-arrow).
projects.exampleText: function sum() { return Array.from(arguments).reduce((a,b) => a+b, 0); }
projects.learningOutcome: Arguments object and its array-like nature.
projects.logicExplanation: The `arguments` object (available in non-arrow functions) is an array-like object containing the passed arguments. It's often replaced by rest parameters in modern code.
projects.codeExplanation: The script uses the `arguments` object to sum any number of arguments, showing its array-like nature.

projects.title: Default Parameters with Object Destructuring
projects.description: Combine default parameters and object destructuring for flexible options.
projects.exampleText: function configure({ port = 3000, host = 'localhost' } = {}) { console.log(port, host); }
projects.logicExplanation: Default parameters can be combined with object destructuring to provide default values for properties, making function calls more flexible.
projects.codeExplanation: The function accepts an options object with destructured properties and default values, allowing optional parameters.

projects.title: Function Composition
projects.description: Combine multiple functions into one using composition.
projects.exampleText: const compose = (f, g) => x => f(g(x));
projects.logicExplanation: Function composition is combining multiple functions into one, where the output of one becomes the input of another. It's a functional programming technique.
projects.codeExplanation: The script defines a compose function that takes two functions and returns a new function that applies them in sequence.

projects.title: Memoization (Caching)
projects.description: Cache function results to avoid recalculating.
projects.exampleText: function memoize(fn) { const cache = {}; return function(...args) { const key = JSON.stringify(args); if (cache[key]) return cache[key]; return cache[key] = fn(...args); }; }
projects.logicExplanation: Memoization caches function results based on input arguments to avoid redundant computations. It's useful for expensive recursive functions.
projects.codeExplanation: The code adds memoization to the Fibonacci function, drastically improving performance for repeated calls.

projects.title: Currying
projects.description: Transform a function that takes multiple arguments into a sequence of unary functions.
projects.exampleText: const curry = (fn) => (a) => (b) => fn(a,b);
projects.logicExplanation: Currying transforms a function that takes multiple arguments into a sequence of functions each taking a single argument.
projects.codeExplanation: The script implements a curried version of a multiplication function and shows how it can be partially applied.

projects.title: Async Functions (Promise basics)
projects.description: Use async/await to handle asynchronous operations.
projects.exampleText: async function fetchData() { return new Promise(resolve => setTimeout(() => resolve('data'), 1000)); }
projects.logicExplanation: Async functions return a Promise and allow using `await` to pause execution until a Promise resolves. They simplify asynchronous code.
projects.codeExplanation: The code defines an async function that simulates fetching data with `setTimeout` and returns a Promise, then uses `await` to get the result.

projects.title: setTimeout and setInterval with Functions
projects.description: Schedule function execution with timers.
projects.exampleText: setTimeout(() => console.log('timeout'), 1000);
projects.logicExplanation: `setTimeout` and `setInterval` schedule function execution after a delay. They are used for timers, animations, and periodic tasks.
projects.codeExplanation: The script shows a timeout that logs after 2 seconds and an interval that increments a counter every second, then clears it.

projects.title: Function Binding in Event Handlers
projects.description: Preserve this context when attaching event handlers.
projects.exampleText: button.addEventListener('click', this.handleClick.bind(this));
projects.exampleOutput: this correctly refers to the component
projects.logicExplanation: When event handlers are passed as callbacks, the value of `this` may change. Using `bind` ensures the correct context.
projects.codeExplanation: The code simulates a button click handler, showing how `this` would be lost and how `bind` preserves it.

projects.title: Function Generators (Intro)
projects.description: Create generator functions that yield values lazily.
projects.exampleText: function* idGenerator() { let id = 1; while(true) { yield id++; } }
projects.logicExplanation: Generator functions (`function*`) can yield multiple values over time and pause execution, producing an iterator. They are used for lazy sequences and async flows.
projects.codeExplanation: The script defines a generator that yields numbers 1 to 3, and iterates over it using `for...of`.

projects.title: Throttling and Debouncing (Concept)
projects.description: Implement throttle and debounce functions to limit execution frequency.
projects.exampleText: function throttle(fn, delay) { let lastCall = 0; return function(...args) { ... }; }
projects.exampleOutput: Logs at most once per delay
projects.logicExplanation: Throttling limits how often a function can be called (e.g., once per 100ms), while debouncing delays execution until after a pause.
projects.codeExplanation: The code provides simple implementations of throttle and debounce, and demonstrates their effect with repeated calls.

projects.title: Function with Rest and Spread Combined
projects.description: Use rest to collect arguments and spread to pass them to another function.
projects.exampleText: function logAll(...args) { console.log(...args); }
projects.logicExplanation: Rest and spread can be combined to accept a variable number of arguments and then pass them to another function.
projects.codeExplanation: The script defines a function that uses rest to collect arguments, then uses spread to call another function with them.

projects.title: Error Handling in Functions (try/catch)
projects.description: Use try/catch to handle errors gracefully inside functions.
projects.exampleText: function safeParse(json) { try { return JSON.parse(json); } catch { return null; } }
projects.logicExplanation: `try...catch` blocks allow handling errors gracefully inside functions, preventing crashes and enabling recovery.
projects.codeExplanation: The function attempts a risky operation (like parsing invalid JSON) and catches the error, returning a default value instead.

projects.title: Comprehensive Function Practice: Utility Library
projects.description: Build a small utility library using many function concepts: composition, memoization, throttling, etc.
projects.exampleText: const utils = { compose, memoize, throttle, ... }
projects.exampleOutput: A working set of utility functions.
projects.logicExplanation: This comprehensive project combines multiple function concepts into a utility library. It includes pure functions, closures, higher-order functions, error handling, and more, demonstrating real-world usage.
projects.codeExplanation: The code defines a set of utility functions (e.g., compose, memoize, throttle) and uses them to build a small application, showcasing integration of various function features.
